title: College & Young Adulthood
subtitle: What the Bible Says About Growing Up and Finding Your Way
summary: Scripture speaks to young adults with both high expectation and tender encouragement, calling them to seek wisdom early, resist conformity, and build their lives on foundations that last.
body:
The transition to adulthood is a season of identity formation, and Scripture takes it seriously. Paul tells Timothy not to let anyone look down on him because he is young, but to set an example in speech, conduct, love, faith, and purity [verse:1tim.4.12]. Youth is not a season to wait through — it is a season to live with intention.

The Preacher in Ecclesiastes addresses young people directly: remember your Creator in the days of your youth [verse:eccl.12.1]. The invitation is to build a relationship with God before the pressures of later life crowd it out. Foundations laid early are foundations that hold.

Proverbs opens with a father's counsel to his son: trust in the Lord with all your heart and lean not on your own understanding [verse:prov.3.5-6]. The college years are filled with new ideas, competing worldviews, and pressure to conform. Paul warns against conformity and urges transformation through renewed thinking [verse:rom.12.2].

Daniel models faithfulness under cultural pressure. Deported to Babylon as a young man, surrounded by a culture that contradicted everything he believed, he resolved not to defile himself [verse:dan.1.8]. His faith did not make him inflexible — he excelled in Babylonian learning — but it gave him a core that could not be displaced.

Jeremiah was called to prophetic ministry as a young man. When he protested that he was too young, God responded: do not say I am too young. I will be with you [verse:jer.1.7-8]. The call to significance does not wait for a certain age.

verses.annotation: Paul tells Timothy to set an example despite his youth — age is no barrier to faithfulness.
Remember your Creator while you are young — build the foundation before life fills in.
Trust in the Lord and do not rely solely on your own understanding — wisdom for the uncertain.
Do not conform to this world's pattern — transformation through renewed thinking.
Daniel's resolve not to compromise despite immense cultural pressure — conviction in a foreign land.
God overrides Jeremiah's objection of youth — calling is not gated by age.
How can a young person stay on the path of purity? By living according to God's word.

scholars.quote: Towner reads 1 Timothy 4:12 as Paul commissioning Timothy to lead not despite his youth but through it — the quality of his example, not his age, would earn the community's trust.
scholars.source: The Letters to Timothy and Titus (Towner, 2006)

scholars.quote: Garrett interprets Ecclesiastes 12:1 as the Teacher's most urgent pastoral counsel — youth's energy and freedom make it the ideal season for establishing the habits of devotion that sustain a lifetime.
Collins argues that Daniel 1 presents a model of cultural engagement without capitulation — Daniel absorbed Babylonian learning while maintaining the ethical boundaries that defined his identity.
